title: The Stack That Wouldn't Move
summary: A deployment stack won't update, won't roll back, and won't delete. Something vanished over the weekend.

personality.role: senior engineer brought in to diagnose the stuck stack
personality.demeanor: dry, reports stack status the way a doctor reads test results
personality.recurring_concern: four pull requests queued, one fixes billing bug overcharging enterprise customers since Tuesday

facts: four pull requests queued behind the stuck stack
one is a fix for a billing calculation bug overcharging enterprise customers since Tuesday
product manager has asked about it twice today
someone on the team mentions CI/CD role permissions were tightened last month
they wonder if the deploy role cannot modify RDS resources anymore
IAM policy is attached to the artifacts if you want to check
the developer who deleted the database (Marcus Chen) is on PTO this week
his Slack status says 'hiking in Patagonia', set Saturday morning
does not match what stack events are showing
stack is not failing on permissions or configuration
CloudFormation is trying to act on a resource and cannot find it
look at which specific resource is causing the rollback failure

glossary.CloudFormation stack: A collection of AWS resources that CloudFormation creates and manages as a single unit based on a template file. When you update the template, CloudFormation figures out what changed and modifies only those resources.
glossary.Stack status: A label that tells you what CloudFormation is currently doing with a stack. Common statuses include CREATE_COMPLETE (stack built successfully), UPDATE_IN_PROGRESS (changes being applied), and UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED (a rollback that could not finish).
glossary.UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED: A stuck state. CloudFormation tried to undo a failed update but could not restore one or more resources to their previous state. The stack cannot accept new updates until you resolve the stuck rollback.
glossary.Logical ID: The name you give a resource inside a CloudFormation template, like ProdDatabase. CloudFormation maps this to the actual AWS resource (the physical ID) it created.
glossary.Drift detection: A CloudFormation feature that compares what the template says a resource should look like with what the resource actually looks like in AWS. If someone changed the resource outside CloudFormation, drift detection reports the difference.
glossary.Stack policy: A JSON document attached to a stack that restricts which resources CloudFormation itself can update or delete. It protects against accidental template changes but does not prevent someone from modifying resources directly in the AWS Console.
glossary.ContinueUpdateRollback: An API call that tells CloudFormation to retry a stuck rollback. The key parameter is --resources-to-skip, which lets CloudFormation finish the rollback by ignoring resources that no longer exist or cannot be restored.
glossary.Service Control Policy (SCP): An organization-level policy that sets permission guardrails for all accounts underneath it. An SCP can prevent specific API calls (like deleting an RDS instance) for all users in an account, regardless of their individual IAM permissions.
glossary.CloudTrail: A service that records every API call made in your AWS account -- who did what, when, and from where. It is the audit trail you check when something was changed and you need to find out who changed it.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player opens stack events, enters the gap between what template says should exist and what actually exists -- usual deployment troubleshooting does not apply, stack is not failing on code or permissions
narrative_arc.trials: IAM permissions on deploy role tightened last month (red herring), stack events point to ProdDatabase, CloudTrail shows DeleteDBInstance by mchen on Friday 4:47 PM, developer on PTO in Patagonia
narrative_arc.revelation: CloudFormation maintains its own model of the world -- Marcus deleted RDS instance through console, CloudFormation did not know, rollback tried to restore a resource that no longer existed, stack froze
narrative_arc.return: continue-update-rollback with --resources-to-skip ProdDatabase, stack returns to UPDATE_ROLLBACK_COMPLETE, deployments resume, team adds drift detection, stack policy, and SCP

components.name: CodePipeline (threadline-prod-pipeline)
components.role: Watches the main branch on GitHub, runs CodeBuild, then triggers a CloudFormation UpdateStack on threadline-prod-stack
components.failure_impact: If the stack is in a non-updatable state, every pipeline execution fails immediately at the deploy stage

components.name: CloudFormation Stack (threadline-prod-stack)
components.role: Manages all production resources as a single unit: EC2 auto-scaling group, RDS instance, three Lambda functions, SQS queue, and IAM roles
components.failure_impact: If the stack enters UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED, no resource in the stack can be updated through the pipeline until the rollback is resolved

components.name: RDS PostgreSQL (threadline-prod-db / ProdDatabase)
components.role: Production database storing all Threadline application data, managed by CloudFormation under logical ID ProdDatabase
components.failure_impact: If deleted outside CloudFormation, the stack's internal state model becomes stale. The next rollback that references this resource will fail.

components.name: IAM Deploy Role (threadline-deploy-role)
components.role: Assumed by CodePipeline during the deploy stage to execute CloudFormation operations
components.failure_impact: If the role lacks permissions, stack updates fail with AccessDenied. In this incident, the role had full permissions -- it was a red herring.

components.name: Lambda Functions (webhook, processor, notifier)
components.role: Three application functions managed by the stack. The webhook function update triggered the original failure due to a deprecated nodejs16.x runtime.
components.failure_impact: The Lambda update failure was the trigger for the rollback, but not the root cause of the stuck state

components.name: CloudTrail
components.role: Records all API calls in the account, including the DeleteDBInstance call that caused the incident
components.failure_impact: Without CloudTrail, there would be no way to determine who deleted the RDS instance or when

system_narration.data_flow: A merged pull request triggers CodePipeline, which builds artifacts in CodeBuild and uploads a new template to S3. CodePipeline then calls CloudFormation UpdateStack using threadline-deploy-role. CloudFormation compares the new template with its internal state, computes a changeset, and begins updating resources in dependency order. If any resource update fails, CloudFormation initiates a rollback, restoring each resource to its prior state. During rollback, CloudFormation reads its state model to determine what each resource should look like and issues the necessary API calls to restore it.
system_narration.what_broke: On Friday at 4:47 PM, IAM user mchen deleted the RDS instance threadline-prod-db directly from the AWS Console. CloudFormation was not involved and did not update its state model. On Monday at 9:12 AM, a routine pipeline execution triggered a stack update. The Lambda webhook function failed to update due to a deprecated runtime, and CloudFormation initiated a rollback. During rollback, CloudFormation attempted to restore ProdDatabase to its previous configuration, but the underlying RDS instance no longer existed. The rollback failed on that resource, and the stack entered UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED. Every subsequent deployment attempt was rejected immediately because the stack cannot accept updates in this state.

hints.hint: look at the stack events -- which resource failed during rollback?
does that resource still exist? check if it was modified outside CloudFormation
CloudFormation tried to roll back to the previous state but the resource it expected to find was gone
aws cloudformation continue-update-rollback can skip resources that no longer exist
use --resources-to-skip to tell CloudFormation to ignore the deleted RDS instance during rollback

resolution.root_cause: A CloudFormation-managed RDS instance (threadline-prod-db) was manually deleted from the AWS Console on Friday afternoon. When the next stack update ran on Monday, the update failed and CloudFormation attempted to roll back, but could not restore the deleted RDS instance, leaving the stack in UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the RDS instance threadline-prod-db was manually deleted outside of CloudFormation, causing the rollback failure.
Player uses aws cloudformation continue-update-rollback with --resources-to-skip to skip the deleted RDS instance and complete the rollback.
Player explains why manual changes to CloudFormation-managed resources break the stack's state model.
Player proposes drift detection, stack policies, or SCPs to prevent manual modification of CloudFormation-managed resources.

learning_objectives: CloudFormation maintains its own state model of resources. Deleting a resource outside CloudFormation creates a mismatch between expected and actual state.
UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED occurs when CloudFormation cannot roll back to the previous state, typically because a resource it needs to restore no longer exists.
ContinueUpdateRollback with --resources-to-skip allows CloudFormation to complete a stuck rollback by skipping resources that cannot be restored.
Drift detection identifies differences between the expected and actual configuration of stack resources.
Stack policies can protect critical resources from accidental updates or deletions through CloudFormation itself, but cannot prevent console-level manual changes.
sop_steps: 1. Confirm the stack is stuck. Run aws cloudformation describe-stacks --stack-name threadline-prod-stack and look at the StackStatus field. If it says UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED, the stack tried to undo a failed update but got stuck partway through. No further updates are possible until this is resolved.

2. Find out which resource caused the jam. Run aws cloudformation describe-stack-events --stack-name threadline-prod-stack to see the history of what happened. Look for resources with UPDATE_FAILED status -- these are the ones CloudFormation could not restore during the rollback.

3. Read the failure reason. The failed resource is ProdDatabase (the production database), and the status reason says 'DBInstance threadline-prod-db not found.' This means CloudFormation tried to restore the database to its previous state, but the database no longer exists.

4. Find out who deleted the database. Check CloudTrail (the AWS audit log that records every API call) for a DeleteDBInstance event. This reveals who deleted the database, when, and whether they used the web console or the CLI.
5. Confirm the database is truly gone. Run aws rds describe-db-instances --db-instance-identifier threadline-prod-db. If it returns a 'not found' error, the database no longer exists in the account.

6. Tell CloudFormation to finish the rollback without the missing database. Run aws cloudformation continue-update-rollback --stack-name threadline-prod-stack --resources-to-skip ProdDatabase. The --resources-to-skip flag tells CloudFormation: 'skip this resource and finish the rollback as if it were fine.' This unsticks the stack.

7. Wait for the stack to reach UPDATE_ROLLBACK_COMPLETE. Check progress with aws cloudformation describe-stacks --stack-name threadline-prod-stack. Once it reaches this status, the stack can accept new updates again.
8. Verify the deployment pipeline is unblocked by triggering a test deployment or confirming the stack accepts a new update.

9. Recreate the database. Update the CloudFormation template and run a stack update to create a new RDS instance. Restore data from the most recent automated snapshot (a backup AWS takes automatically).

10. Set up automatic change detection. CloudFormation has a feature called drift detection that compares what the template says should exist with what actually exists in AWS. Schedule it to run daily using an EventBridge rule (a scheduler) that triggers a Lambda function calling aws cloudformation detect-stack-drift. This catches manual changes before they cause rollback failures.

11. Protect critical resources from accidental deletion. Apply a stack policy -- a set of rules attached to the stack that restricts which resources CloudFormation itself can delete. This adds a safety check when someone changes the template.

12. Prevent manual deletions in the production account. Ask the platform team to add a Service Control Policy (SCP) -- an organization-level rule that restricts who can call rds:DeleteDBInstance in the production account. Limit it to the CI/CD role only, so individuals cannot delete production databases from the web console.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Someone changes a resource's settings through the console instead of deleting it
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of deleting the database, someone changes its size or settings directly in the AWS web console (for example, switching it from a large to a small instance type). CloudFormation does not know this happened. The next stack update may succeed but produce unexpected behavior because the real resource no longer matches what the template describes. If the mismatch is severe enough to conflict with a rollback, the stack can still get stuck. Drift detection (the feature that compares expected vs actual state) would catch this before it becomes a problem.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Schedule daily drift detection and alert whenever a resource's actual state does not match the template. Use Service Control Policies to restrict who can modify CloudFormation-managed resources through the console.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The deploy role's permissions were changed between updates, so the rollback cannot complete
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The role that CloudFormation uses to make changes lost a permission it had during the last successful update. When a new update fails and CloudFormation tries to undo it, it cannot perform the required actions because the role no longer has the right permissions. The stack gets stuck with 'AccessDenied' errors instead of 'resource not found' errors.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Track changes to the CloudFormation execution role using CloudTrail (the audit log). Use a dedicated role for CloudFormation and avoid modifying it outside of the infrastructure-as-code pipeline.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A stack-within-a-stack (nested stack) gets stuck independently
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: CloudFormation lets you organize resources into nested stacks -- a parent stack that contains child stacks. A child stack can get stuck in UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED while the parent is still rolling back. You must find the failed resources in the child stack specifically and use a special format (NestedStackName.ResourceLogicalID) when telling CloudFormation which resources to skip. Using the wrong format produces an error.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Organize stacks by lifecycle and ownership so that problems in one area do not block everything. Test nested stack updates in a staging environment that mirrors the production setup.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A Lambda function uses a programming language version that AWS no longer supports, blocking the rollback
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: A Lambda function in the stack uses a runtime (like Node.js 16) that AWS has retired. An unrelated update triggers a rollback, but CloudFormation cannot restore the Lambda function to its previous settings because the old runtime is no longer valid. This is actually what triggered the initial failure in this sim, but on its own it can also cause the stack to get stuck.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Keep Lambda runtimes updated before AWS retires them. Use template validation tools (like cfn-lint or CloudFormation Hooks) to catch unsupported runtimes before deployment.

sop_practices: Never modify, delete, or create resources by hand if CloudFormation manages them. CloudFormation keeps its own record of what exists. When someone changes a resource outside of CloudFormation, the record becomes wrong, and the next update or rollback can fail in confusing ways.

Run drift detection daily at minimum. Drift detection compares what CloudFormation thinks exists with what actually exists in AWS. Alert whenever a resource shows anything other than 'in sync.' Catching manual changes early prevents them from turning into stuck rollbacks later.

Preview changes before applying them. CloudFormation has a feature called change sets that shows you exactly what will be created, modified, or deleted before you execute an update. Require peer review for change sets that touch critical resources like databases or networking.

Protect critical resources at multiple levels. Apply a stack policy (rules attached to the stack) to prevent CloudFormation from accidentally deleting important resources like databases. Use Service Control Policies (organization-level rules) to prevent individuals from deleting production resources through the web console.
